file.content:
#!/bin/bash
#
# Copyright 2026 Scale Invariant, Inc.
#
# Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
# you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
# You may obtain a copy of the License at
#
#     http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0
#
# Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
# distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
# WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
# See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
# limitations under the License.
#
# Author: Brad Hyslop <bhyslop@scaleinvariant.org>
#
# BUJB CLI - Command line interface for the jurisdiction module
#
# Furnish loads BURC, BURS, BURP (per investiture folio), and the cross-
# referenced BURN profile, then kindles bujb. Command functions call
# bujb_resolve_investiture before doing any work; per-verb platform
# invariants are asserted inline at the command body.

set -euo pipefail

source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buc_command.sh"

######################################################################
# Command Functions

# bujb_resolve - diagnostic: load investiture and print resolved fields.
# No remote action; verifies regime + key-file health for a target.
bujb_resolve() {
  buc_doc_brief "Cross-validate an investiture and display the regime fields it resolves"
  buc_doc_shown || return 0

  test -n "${BUZ_FOLIO:-}" || burp_die_no_folio

  bujb_resolve_investiture

  buc_step "Resolved investiture ${BUZ_FOLIO}:"
  buc_bare "  BURP_VICEROYALTY        = ${BURP_VICEROYALTY}"
  buc_bare "  BURN_HOST               = ${BURN_HOST}"
  buc_bare "  BURN_PLATFORM           = ${BURN_PLATFORM}"
  buc_bare "  BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER    = ${BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER}"
  buc_bare "  BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE= ${BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE}"
  buc_bare "  BURP_WORKLOAD_KEY_FILE  = ${BURP_WORKLOAD_KEY_FILE}"
  buc_bare "  BURC_WORKLOAD_USER      = ${BURC_WORKLOAD_USER}"
}

# bujb_knock - probe workload reachability (workload SSH + remote no-op).
bujb_knock() {
  buc_doc_brief "Probe workload SSH reachability for an investiture"
  buc_doc_shown || return 0

  test -n "${BUZ_FOLIO:-}" || burp_die_no_folio

  bujb_resolve_investiture

  buc_step "Knocking ${BURC_WORKLOAD_USER}@${BURN_HOST} (${BURP_VICEROYALTY})"

  ssh -i "${BURP_WORKLOAD_KEY_FILE}"     \
      -o IdentitiesOnly=yes                       \
      -o BatchMode=yes                            \
      -o StrictHostKeyChecking=accept-new         \
      -o ConnectTimeout=10                        \
      "${BURC_WORKLOAD_USER}@${BURN_HOST}" \
      true                                        \
    || buc_die "Knock failed for ${BURP_VICEROYALTY}"

  buc_step "Knock succeeded"
}

# bujb_command_file - stream a local command file's contents to the workload
# remote shell. Captures stdout/stderr/exit to BURD_OUTPUT_DIR. Per spec:
# returns 0 when dispatch + capture complete (independent of remote exit);
# non-zero on SSH-level failure (exit 255).
bujb_command_file() {
  buc_doc_brief "Execute a local command file as workload; capture stdout/stderr/exit"
  buc_doc_param "command_file" "Local file streamed as the remote command body"
  buc_doc_shown || return 0

  test -n "${BUZ_FOLIO:-}" || burp_die_no_folio

  local z_command_file="${1:-}"
  test -n "${z_command_file}"   || buc_usage_die
  test -f "${z_command_file}"   || buc_die "Command file not found: ${z_command_file}"
  test -r "${z_command_file}"   || buc_die "Command file not readable: ${z_command_file}"

  bujb_resolve_investiture

  buc_step "Streaming ${z_command_file} to ${BURC_WORKLOAD_USER}@${BURN_HOST}"
  buc_step "Output dir: ${BURD_OUTPUT_DIR}"

  local z_exit=0
  ssh -i "${BURP_WORKLOAD_KEY_FILE}"     \
      -o IdentitiesOnly=yes                       \
      -o BatchMode=yes                            \
      -o StrictHostKeyChecking=accept-new         \
      -o ConnectTimeout=10                        \
      "${BURC_WORKLOAD_USER}@${BURN_HOST}" \
      'bash -s'                                   \
      < "${z_command_file}"                       \
      > "${BURD_OUTPUT_DIR}/stdout.log"           \
      2> "${BURD_OUTPUT_DIR}/stderr.log"          \
    || z_exit=$?

  echo "${z_exit}" > "${BURD_OUTPUT_DIR}/exitcode"

  test "${z_exit}" -ne 255 \
    || buc_die "SSH connection or authentication failed (exit 255). See ${BURD_OUTPUT_DIR}/stderr.log"

  buc_step "Remote exit code: ${z_exit}"
  buc_step "Capture complete"
}

# bujb_fenestrate - fenestrate ceremony (Windows OpenSSH only).
bujb_fenestrate_command() {
  buc_doc_brief "Fenestrate — admin SSH key trust + sshd_config harden (Windows)"
  buc_doc_shown || return 0

  test -n "${BUZ_FOLIO:-}" || burp_die_no_folio
  bujb_resolve_investiture
  bujb_fenestrate
}

# bujb_garrison_bash - garrison ceremony, native bash workload shell (Linux/Mac).
bujb_garrison_bash() {
  buc_doc_brief "Garrison workload account on a bubep_linux/bubep_mac node (shell-letter b)"
  buc_doc_shown || return 0

  test -n "${BUZ_FOLIO:-}" || burp_die_no_folio
  bujb_resolve_investiture
  bujb_garrison b
}

# bujb_garrison_cygwin - garrison ceremony, Cygwin bash workload shell (Windows).
bujb_garrison_cygwin() {
  buc_doc_brief "Garrison workload account on a bubep_windows node via Cygwin (shell-letter c)"
  buc_doc_shown || return 0

  test -n "${BUZ_FOLIO:-}" || burp_die_no_folio
  bujb_resolve_investiture
  bujb_garrison c
}

# bujb_garrison_wsl - garrison ceremony, WSL bash workload shell (Windows).
bujb_garrison_wsl() {
  buc_doc_brief "Garrison workload account on a bubep_windows node via WSL (shell-letter w)"
  buc_doc_shown || return 0

  test -n "${BUZ_FOLIO:-}" || burp_die_no_folio
  bujb_resolve_investiture
  bujb_garrison w
}

# bujb_interactive_session - hand control to ssh as workload.
bujb_interactive_session() {
  buc_doc_brief "Interactive SSH session as workload"
  buc_doc_shown || return 0

  test -n "${BUZ_FOLIO:-}" || burp_die_no_folio

  bujb_resolve_investiture

  buc_step "Opening interactive session: ${BURC_WORKLOAD_USER}@${BURN_HOST} (${BURP_VICEROYALTY})"

  exec ssh -t                                     \
      -i "${BURP_WORKLOAD_KEY_FILE}"     \
      -o IdentitiesOnly=yes                       \
      -o StrictHostKeyChecking=accept-new         \
      "${BURC_WORKLOAD_USER}@${BURN_HOST}" \
      "bash -i"
}

######################################################################
# Furnish and Main

zbujb_furnish() {
  buc_doc_env "BURD_BUK_DIR          " "BUK module directory (dispatch-provided)"
  buc_doc_env "BURD_CONFIG_DIR       " "BUK config directory (dispatch-provided)"
  buc_doc_env_done || return 0

  source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buv_validation.sh"
  source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/burd_regime.sh"
  source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/burc_regime.sh"
  source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/burs_regime.sh"
  source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buf_fact.sh"
  source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/bubc_constants.sh"
  source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/burn_regime.sh"
  source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/burp_regime.sh"
  source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/bupr_PresentationRegime.sh"
  source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/bujb_jurisdiction.sh"

  zbuv_kindle
  zburd_kindle
  zburd_enforce

  source "${BURD_REGIME_FILE}" || buc_die "Failed to source BURC: ${BURD_REGIME_FILE}"

  zburc_kindle
  zburc_enforce

  source "${BURD_STATION_FILE}" || buc_die "Failed to source BURS: ${BURD_STATION_FILE}"

  zburs_kindle
  zburs_enforce

  zbupr_kindle
  zbujb_kindle

  test -n "${BUZ_FOLIO:-}" || burp_die_no_folio

  local -r z_burp_file="${BURD_CONFIG_DIR}/${BUBC_rbmu_users_subdir}/${BURS_USER}/${BUZ_FOLIO}/burp.env"
  test -f "${z_burp_file}" || buc_die "BURP profile not found: ${z_burp_file}"
  source "${z_burp_file}"  || buc_die "Failed to source BURP: ${z_burp_file}"
  zburp_kindle
  zburp_enforce

  # Cross-reference: BURP_VICEROYALTY must resolve to a registered BURN profile.
  local -r z_burn_file="${BURD_CONFIG_DIR}/${BUBC_rbmn_nodes_subdir}/${BURP_VICEROYALTY}/burn.env"
  test -f "${z_burn_file}" || buc_die "BURN profile referenced by BURP_VICEROYALTY=${BURP_VICEROYALTY} not found: ${z_burn_file}"
  source "${z_burn_file}"  || buc_die "Failed to source BURN: ${z_burn_file}"
  zburn_kindle
  zburn_enforce
}

buc_execute bujb_ "Bash Utility Jurisdiction" zbujb_furnish "$@"

# eof

1	//axhob_operation
2	{bust_garrison_bash}
3	
4	NOTE: {busn_garrison} ceremony with native bash as the workload shell,
5	for Linux and macOS nodes.
6	Workload-only: garrison destroys any prior workload account named
7	{burc_workload_user}
8	and recreates it from a clean slate.
9	Garrison never reads or writes any `sshd_config` and never modifies
10	admin authorized_keys.
11	
12	//axhopt_typed_parameter
13	{burp_investiture}
14	
15	//axhoq_precondition
16	A {burp_regime} profile exists at the given {burp_investiture} and its
17	referenced {burn_regime} profile is reachable.
18	
19	//axhoq_precondition
20	sshd is reachable on the node and {burp_privileged_user} authenticates
21	by key alone using {burp_privileged_key_file}. On Linux/Mac this
22	admin trust is operator-managed (e.g., placed via `ssh-copy-id`) —
23	there is no `busn_fenestrate` for non-Windows-OpenSSH nodes.
24	
25	//axhoq_precondition
26	{burp_workload_key_file} is unencrypted and exists on the operator's
27	station.
28	
29	//axhos_step
30	. *Open admin SSH*: connect as {burp_privileged_user} using
31	{burp_privileged_key_file}; key-auth only. Failure here surfaces a
32	missing or stale admin trust.
33	
34	//axhos_step
35	. *Destroy workload*: remove any existing {burc_workload_user} account
36	via `userdel -r`.
37	
38	//axhos_step
39	. *Create workload*: provision {burc_workload_user} as an unprivileged,
40	ssh-only account.
41	
42	//axhos_step
43	. *Place workload trust*: write the workload pubkey
44	(derived via `ssh-keygen -y`) to the workload's authorized_keys with
45	the shell-letter `b` `command=` directive from {bujb_jurisdiction}.
46	
47	//axhos_step
48	. *Plant workload key*: copy {burp_workload_key_file} to the workload
49	account at the shell-letter `b` destination path hardcoded in
50	{bujb_jurisdiction}.
51	
52	//axhos_step
53	. *Validate round-trip*: SSH as workload, run a no-op command, verify
54	exit 0.
55	
56	//axhog_guarantee
57	On success the node has exactly one workload account named
58	{burc_workload_user}, ssh-only, with both inbound (from operator) and
59	outbound (to GitHub or equivalent) key trust in place.
60	
61	//axhoc_completion
62	Exit 0 when the round-trip validation succeeds.
63	Non-zero on admin SSH failure, account-management failure, key-write
64	failure, or workload validation failure.
65

lastPrompt: mount A-    Review pace ₢A-AAm "implement-jurisdiction" against its docket and paddock.   Do NOT wrap, do NOT make code changes. Read-only review with a written   findings report at the end.    ## Wha…

1	//axhob_operation
2	{bust_garrison_cygwin}
3	
4	NOTE: {busn_garrison} ceremony with Cygwin bash as the workload shell,
5	for Windows nodes reached via Cygwin sshd.
6	Workload-only: garrison destroys any prior workload account named
7	{burc_workload_user}
8	and recreates it from a clean slate.
9	Garrison never reads or writes any `sshd_config` and never modifies
10	admin authorized_keys; admin trust is established beforehand by
11	{busn_fenestrate}
12	on the Windows OpenSSH server, while Cygwin sshd hardening (if used
13	in addition to Windows OpenSSH) remains operator-manual.
14	
15	//axhopt_typed_parameter
16	{burp_investiture}
17	
18	//axhoq_precondition
19	A {burp_regime} profile exists at the given {burp_investiture} and its
20	referenced {burn_regime} profile is reachable.
21	
22	//axhoq_precondition
23	{busn_fenestrate} has run for this {burp_investiture} so that
24	{burp_privileged_user} can authenticate by key alone using
25	{burp_privileged_key_file} (Windows OpenSSH path). Cygwin sshd is
26	installed on the node.
27	
28	//axhoq_precondition
29	{burp_workload_key_file} is unencrypted and exists on the operator's
30	station.
31	
32	//axhos_step
33	. *Open admin SSH*: connect as {burp_privileged_user} using
34	{burp_privileged_key_file}; key-auth only. Failure here surfaces a
35	missing or stale admin trust.
36	
37	//axhos_step
38	. *Destroy workload*: remove any existing {burc_workload_user} Cygwin
39	account via the Cygwin user-management equivalent of `userdel -r`.
40	
41	//axhos_step
42	. *Create workload*: provision {burc_workload_user} as an unprivileged,
43	ssh-only Cygwin account.
44	
45	//axhos_step
46	. *Place workload trust*: write the workload pubkey
47	(derived via `ssh-keygen -y`) to the workload's authorized_keys with
48	the shell-letter `c` `command=` directive from {bujb_jurisdiction}.
49	
50	//axhos_step
51	. *Plant workload key*: copy {burp_workload_key_file} to the workload
52	account at the shell-letter `c` destination path hardcoded in
53	{bujb_jurisdiction}.
54	
55	//axhos_step
56	. *Validate round-trip*: SSH as workload, run a no-op command, verify
57	exit 0.
58	
59	//axhog_guarantee
60	On success the node has exactly one workload account named
61	{burc_workload_user} reachable via Cygwin bash.
62	
63	//axhoc_completion
64	Exit 0 when the round-trip validation succeeds.
65	Non-zero on admin SSH failure, Cygwin account-management failure,
66	key-write failure, or workload validation failure.
67

1	//axhob_operation
2	{bust_garrison_wsl}
3	
4	NOTE: {busn_garrison} ceremony with WSL bash as the workload shell,
5	for Windows nodes reached via the WSL distribution `rbtww-main`
6	(hardcoded in {bujb_jurisdiction}).
7	Workload-only: garrison destroys any prior workload account named
8	{burc_workload_user}
9	and recreates it from a clean slate.
10	Garrison never reads or writes any `sshd_config` and never modifies
11	admin authorized_keys; admin trust is established beforehand by
12	{busn_fenestrate}
13	on the Windows OpenSSH server, while sshd hardening inside the WSL
14	distribution remains operator-manual.
15	
16	//axhopt_typed_parameter
17	{burp_investiture}
18	
19	//axhoq_precondition
20	A {burp_regime} profile exists at the given {burp_investiture} and its
21	referenced {burn_regime} profile is reachable.
22	
23	//axhoq_precondition
24	The default WSL distribution on the node is `rbtww-main` and its sshd
25	is installed; mismatch fails fast with a clear error.
26	
27	//axhoq_precondition
28	{busn_fenestrate} has run for this {burp_investiture} so that
29	{burp_privileged_user} can authenticate by key alone using
30	{burp_privileged_key_file} into the Windows OpenSSH host (used as the
31	admin entry point); the WSL session is then opened from that
32	foothold.
33	
34	//axhoq_precondition
35	{burp_workload_key_file} is unencrypted and exists on the operator's
36	station.
37	
38	//axhos_step
39	. *Open admin SSH*: connect as {burp_privileged_user} using
40	{burp_privileged_key_file}; key-auth only into the WSL distribution.
41	Failure here surfaces a missing or stale admin trust.
42	
43	//axhos_step
44	. *Destroy workload*: remove any existing {burc_workload_user} account
45	inside the WSL distribution via `userdel -r`.
46	
47	//axhos_step
48	. *Create workload*: provision {burc_workload_user} as an unprivileged,
49	ssh-only Linux account inside `rbtww-main`.
50	
51	//axhos_step
52	. *Place workload trust*: write the workload pubkey
53	(derived via `ssh-keygen -y`) to the workload's authorized_keys with
54	the shell-letter `w` `command=` directive from {bujb_jurisdiction}.
55	
56	//axhos_step
57	. *Plant workload key*: copy {burp_workload_key_file} to the workload
58	account at the shell-letter `w` destination path hardcoded in
59	{bujb_jurisdiction}.
60	
61	//axhos_step
62	. *Validate round-trip*: SSH as workload, run a no-op command, verify
63	exit 0.
64	
65	//axhog_guarantee
66	On success the node has exactly one workload account named
67	{burc_workload_user} reachable via the WSL distribution `rbtww-main`.
68	
69	//axhoc_completion
70	Exit 0 when the round-trip validation succeeds.
71	Non-zero on WSL distribution mismatch, admin SSH failure, account-
72	management failure, key-write failure, or workload validation failure.
73

content.text: Let me batch-update tasks and continue the spec review.

content.content: Updated task #1 status
Updated task #2 status
Updated task #4 status

1	//axhob_operation
2	{bust_fenestrate}
3	
4	NOTE: {busn_fenestrate} ceremony — Windows OpenSSH only. Establishes
5	admin SSH key trust and hardens `sshd_config` to key-only
6	authentication on the node, leaving it ready for {busn_garrison} to
7	provision the workload account. Two-phase remote-run because
8	`Restart-Service sshd` terminates its own ssh session: phase 1 places
9	admin trust, writes the hardened `sshd_config`, validates it, and
10	restarts sshd; phase 2 reconnects via key auth alone to verify the
11	post-restart state.
12	
13	//axhopt_typed_parameter
14	{burp_investiture}
15	
16	//axhoq_precondition
17	A {burp_regime} profile exists at the given {burp_investiture} and its
18	referenced {burn_regime} profile targets a Windows OpenSSH node.
19	
20	//axhoq_precondition
21	The Windows handbook bootstrap is complete: OpenSSH Server installed,
22	sshd started and set to Automatic, firewall opens the configured SSH
23	port, and `sshd_config` carries `PasswordAuthentication yes` so that
24	fenestrate's phase-1 admin SSH session can fall through to a password
25	prompt on a fresh node.
26	
27	//axhoq_precondition
28	{burp_privileged_user} is a member of the Administrators group with a
29	known password (typed once on phase-1 first run when ssh prompts on
30	/dev/tty; fenestrate itself never sees the password).
31	
32	//axhoq_precondition
33	{burp_privileged_key_file} is unencrypted and exists on the operator's
34	station.
35	
36	//axhos_step
37	. *Phase 1 — open admin SSH*: connect as {burp_privileged_user} with
38	`PreferredAuthentications=publickey,password`. Steady-state:
39	{burp_privileged_key_file} authenticates by key. First run: ssh
40	prompts on /dev/tty for the admin password; operator types it once.
41	
42	//axhos_step
43	. *Phase 1 — place admin trust*: idempotently install the admin pubkey
44	(derived from {burp_privileged_key_file} via `ssh-keygen -y`) in
45	Windows OpenSSH `administrators_authorized_keys` as a bare key entry
46	(no `command=` directive — fenestrate is uniform across shell-letters).
47	Re-runs converge as a no-op when the line is already present. Performed
48	before any sshd_config change so that key auth is available for
49	phase 2's reconnect.
50	
51	//axhos_step
52	. *Phase 1 — lock down administrators_authorized_keys*: apply
53	`icacls` so only SYSTEM and the Administrators group hold ACL
54	entries; Windows OpenSSH refuses to read the file otherwise.
55	Idempotent.
56	
57	//axhos_step
58	. *Phase 1 — write hardened directives*: set `PubkeyAuthentication yes`,
59	`PasswordAuthentication no`, `PermitEmptyPasswords no` in
60	`sshd_config`. Idempotent — already-hardened configs converge to the
61	same byte sequence on re-run.
62	
63	//axhos_step
64	. *Phase 1 — verify written state*: PowerShell `Get-Content` returns
65	the raw `sshd_config` bytes; bash-side BCG-compliant matching extracts
66	each effective directive value and asserts it matches the intended
67	hardened value. Keeps PowerShell out of the parsing business (its
68	error semantics are unreliable).
69	
70	//axhos_step
71	. *Phase 1 — validate sshd_config*: `sshd -t` runs as the penultimate
72	atomic op of phase 1. A malformed config aborts fenestrate before it
73	could brick the running sshd.
74	
75	//axhos_step
76	. *Phase 1 — restart sshd*: `Restart-Service sshd` is the final atomic
77	op of phase 1. The bash orchestrator treats the disconnect immediately
78	after this exec as the expected phase boundary, not a failure.
79	
80	//axhos_step
81	. *Phase 2 — reconnect via key auth*: re-establish admin SSH as
82	{burp_privileged_user} with `PreferredAuthentications=publickey`
83	(no password fallback). Failure here surfaces a brick — the operator's
84	manual password no longer opens a session.
85	
86	//axhos_step
87	. *Phase 2 — verify post-state*: confirm the post-restart admin
88	session authenticates by key and the running sshd serves the hardened
89	configuration.
90	
91	//axhog_guarantee
92	On success the node has admin SSH hardened to key-only with the
93	operator's admin pubkey installed in `administrators_authorized_keys`
94	under correct ACLs, `sshd_config` carries the three hardening
95	directives, and the post-restart admin session has been verified to
96	authenticate by key without password fallback.
97	
98	//axhoc_completion
99	Exit 0 when phase 2 verification succeeds.
100	Non-zero on phase-1 admin SSH failure, pubkey-write failure, ACL
101	lockdown failure, sshd_config write failure, parsed-state mismatch,
102	`sshd -t` failure, or phase-2 key-auth failure.
103

file.content:
//axhob_operation
{bust_fenestrate}

NOTE: {busn_fenestrate} ceremony — Windows OpenSSH only. Establishes
admin SSH key trust and hardens `sshd_config` to key-only
authentication on the node, leaving it ready for {busn_garrison} to
provision the workload account. Two-phase remote-run because
`Restart-Service sshd` terminates its own ssh session: phase 1 places
admin trust, writes the hardened `sshd_config`, validates it, and
restarts sshd; phase 2 reconnects via key auth alone to verify the
post-restart state.

//axhopt_typed_parameter
{burp_investiture}

//axhoq_precondition
A {burp_regime} profile exists at the given {burp_investiture} and its
referenced {burn_regime} profile targets a Windows OpenSSH node.

//axhoq_precondition
The Windows handbook bootstrap is complete: OpenSSH Server installed,
sshd started and set to Automatic, firewall opens the configured SSH
port, and `sshd_config` carries `PasswordAuthentication yes` so that
fenestrate's phase-1 admin SSH session can fall through to a password
prompt on a fresh node.

//axhoq_precondition
{burp_privileged_user} is a member of the Administrators group with a
known password (typed once on phase-1 first run when ssh prompts on
/dev/tty; fenestrate itself never sees the password).

//axhoq_precondition
{burp_privileged_key_file} is unencrypted and exists on the operator's
station.

//axhos_step
. *Phase 1 — open admin SSH*: connect as {burp_privileged_user} with
`PreferredAuthentications=publickey,password`. Steady-state:
{burp_privileged_key_file} authenticates by key. First run: ssh
prompts on /dev/tty for the admin password; operator types it once.

//axhos_step
. *Phase 1 — place admin trust*: idempotently install the admin pubkey
(derived from {burp_privileged_key_file} via `ssh-keygen -y`) in
Windows OpenSSH `administrators_authorized_keys` as a bare key entry
(no `command=` directive — fenestrate is uniform across shell-letters).
Re-runs converge as a no-op when the line is already present. Performed
before any sshd_config change so that key auth is available for
phase 2's reconnect.

//axhos_step
. *Phase 1 — lock down administrators_authorized_keys*: apply
`icacls` so only SYSTEM and the Administrators group hold ACL
entries; Windows OpenSSH refuses to read the file otherwise.
Idempotent.

//axhos_step
. *Phase 1 — write hardened directives*: set `PubkeyAuthentication yes`,
`PasswordAuthentication no`, `PermitEmptyPasswords no` in
`sshd_config`. Idempotent — already-hardened configs converge to the
same byte sequence on re-run.

//axhos_step
. *Phase 1 — verify written state*: PowerShell `Get-Content` returns
the raw `sshd_config` bytes; bash-side BCG-compliant matching extracts
each effective directive value and asserts it matches the intended
hardened value. Keeps PowerShell out of the parsing business (its
error semantics are unreliable).

//axhos_step
. *Phase 1 — validate sshd_config*: `sshd -t` runs as the penultimate
atomic op of phase 1. A malformed config aborts fenestrate before it
could brick the running sshd.

//axhos_step
. *Phase 1 — restart sshd*: `Restart-Service sshd` is the final atomic
op of phase 1. The bash orchestrator treats the disconnect immediately
after this exec as the expected phase boundary, not a failure.

//axhos_step
. *Phase 2 — reconnect via key auth*: re-establish admin SSH as
{burp_privileged_user} with `PreferredAuthentications=publickey`
(no password fallback). Failure here surfaces a brick — the operator's
manual password no longer opens a session.

//axhos_step
. *Phase 2 — verify post-state*: confirm the post-restart admin
session authenticates by key and the running sshd serves the hardened
configuration.

//axhog_guarantee
On success the node has admin SSH hardened to key-only with the
operator's admin pubkey installed in `administrators_authorized_keys`
under correct ACLs, `sshd_config` carries the three hardening
directives, and the post-restart admin session has been verified to
authenticate by key without password fallback.

//axhoc_completion
Exit 0 when phase 2 verification succeeds.
Non-zero on phase-1 admin SSH failure, pubkey-write failure, ACL
lockdown failure, sshd_config write failure, parsed-state mismatch,
`sshd -t` failure, or phase-2 key-auth failure.

content.content:
1	//axhob_operation
2	{bust_knock}
3	
4	NOTE: Probes the workload account's reachability via key-only SSH.
5	Used to confirm that a {burp_investiture}'s workload trust is currently
6	usable before dispatching real work, and used internally as the
7	final-step validation of {busn_garrison}.
8	
9	//axhopt_typed_parameter
10	{burp_investiture}
11	
12	//axhoq_precondition
13	A {burp_regime} profile exists at the given {burp_investiture} and its
14	referenced {burn_regime} profile is reachable.
15	
16	//axhoq_precondition
17	{busn_garrison} has been run for this {burp_investiture} so that
18	{burc_workload_user} exists on the node and trusts the workload pubkey
19	derived from {burp_workload_key_file}.
20	
21	//axhos_step
22	. *Open workload SSH*: connect as {burc_workload_user} using
23	{burp_workload_key_file}; key-auth only.
24	
25	//axhos_step
26	. *Issue trivial command*: execute a no-op on the remote
27	(e.g., `true`) and observe the remote exit status.
28	
29	//axhog_guarantee
30	No remote OS state was modified.
31	
32	//axhoc_completion
33	Exit 0 when the remote command returns 0 within the probe window.
34	Non-zero on connection refusal, authentication failure, remote-exec
35	failure, or probe-window timeout.
36

1	//axhob_operation
2	{bust_command_file}
3	
4	NOTE: Executes the contents of a local command file on the remote node
5	as {burc_workload_user} and captures stdout, stderr, and the remote
6	exit status to {burd_output_dir}.
7	The remote shell is whichever shell-letter garrisoned the node; the
8	operator does not choose it at dispatch time.
9	
10	//axhopt_typed_parameter
11	{burp_investiture}
12	
13	//axhopt_typed_parameter
14	{busi_scribed_command_file}
15	
16	//axhoq_precondition
17	A {burp_regime} profile exists at the given {burp_investiture} and is
18	{bust_knock}-responsive.
19	
20	//axhoq_precondition
21	The local command file referenced as the dispatch input exists and is
22	readable by the calling user.
23	
24	//axhos_step
25	. *Open workload session*: open an SSH session to {burc_workload_user}
26	using {burp_workload_key_file}.
27	
28	//axhos_step
29	. *Stream command file*: feed the command-file contents to the remote
30	shell.
31	
32	//axhos_step
33	. *Capture outputs*: write remote stdout, stderr, and the remote exit
34	status to {burd_output_dir}.
35	
36	//axhog_guarantee
37	The complete remote stdout, stderr, and exit code are durably written
38	under {burd_output_dir} regardless of the remote command's success or
39	failure.
40	
41	//axhoc_completion
42	Exit 0 when dispatch and capture both complete (independent of the
43	remote exit code).
44	Non-zero on SSH connection failure, capture-write failure, or transport
45	interruption.
46

1	//axhob_operation
2	{bust_interactive_session}
3	
4	NOTE: Opens an interactive SSH session to {burc_workload_user} on the
5	node named by a {burp_investiture}.
6	Foreground operation; control returns to the station shell when the
7	operator exits the remote shell.
8	
9	//axhopt_typed_parameter
10	{burp_investiture}
11	
12	//axhoq_precondition
13	A {burp_regime} profile exists at the given {burp_investiture} and is
14	{bust_knock}-responsive.
15	
16	//axhos_step
17	. *Hand control to SSH*: invoke the local SSH client interactively
18	against {burc_workload_user} using {burp_workload_key_file}.
19	
20	//axhos_step
21	. *Wait for exit*: block until the operator exits the remote shell.
22	
23	//axhog_guarantee
24	No artifact is written to {burd_output_dir} — interactive sessions
25	deliberately bypass dispatch capture.
26	No local profile is modified.
27	
28	//axhoc_completion
29	Exit code mirrors the SSH client's exit code when the session ends
30	normally.
31	Non-zero on connection or authentication failure before the session
32	opens.
33

:burn_regime:                      <<burn_regime,BURN Regime>>

// BURN/BURP Identifier Vocabulary

content.content:
1474	== Remote Node Access
1475	
1476	BUK reaches remote nodes under the
1477	{bus_jurisdiction}
1478	umbrella: a single station user establishes admin SSH trust on the
1479	node, provisions one workload account, and dispatches commands to it.
1480	
1481	[[bus_jurisdiction]]
1482	{bus_jurisdiction}::
1483	The BUK feature-area for reaching remote nodes deterministically.
1484	Two verbs cover the privileged-side ceremonies:
1485	{busn_fenestrate}
1486	hardens admin SSH trust and `sshd_config` on Windows OpenSSH nodes
1487	(operator-manual on Linux/Mac, e.g., `ssh-copy-id`); {busn_garrison}
1488	provisions and refreshes a workload account from an existing admin
1489	SSH foothold. The workload-side tabtargets
1490	({bust_knock}, {bust_command_file}, {bust_interactive_session})
1491	dispatch work to that account.
1492	+
1493	{bus_jurisdiction}
1494	is logical only — there is no AXLA-typed entity behind the umbrella.
1495	Its implementation seat is
1496	{bujb_jurisdiction}.
1497	
1498	[[bujb_jurisdiction]]
1499	{bujb_jurisdiction}::
1500	The BCG-compliant module
1501	(`Tools/buk/bujb_jurisdiction.sh` plus its `bujb_cli.sh` companion)
1502	that houses both
1503	{busn_fenestrate}
1504	and
1505	{busn_garrison}
1506	implementations and hardcodes the
1507	{bus_jurisdiction}
1508	contracts not expressed in regime data:
1509	the three shell-letter→`command=` directive mappings
1510	(`b` = native bash for Linux/Mac;
1511	`c` = Cygwin;
1512	`w` = WSL)
1513	used by garrison on the workload account,
1514	the workload private-key destination paths on the remote per shell-letter,
1515	the canonical WSL distribution name (`rbtww-main`),
1516	and the Windows OpenSSH `sshd_config` hardening directive set used by
1517	fenestrate.
1518	+
1519	SSH transport mechanics live in code;
1520	{burp_regime}
1521	carries no shell selection.
1522	
1523	=== Privileges
1524	
1525	A
1526	{burn_regime}
1527	instance lives under `.buk/rbmn_nodes/<{burn_viceroyalty}>/` and is shared
1528	across station users (git-transported); it carries node shape only and
1529	no key material.
1530	A
1531	{burp_regime}
1532	instance lives under `.buk/rbmu_users/<{burs_user}>/<{burp_investiture}>/`;
1533	the
1534	{burs_user}
1535	owns its credential subtree exclusively, and operations write only
1536	under the current station user's tree.
1537	
1538	{busn_garrison}
1539	authenticates as
1540	{burp_privileged_user}
1541	using
1542	{burp_privileged_key_file}
1543	by key alone; admin trust is established beforehand by
1544	{busn_fenestrate}
1545	on Windows (which uses a one-time password fallback on its own
1546	phase-1 admin SSH session) or operator-manually on Linux/Mac.
1547	Workload account is unprivileged, ssh-only, and named by
1548	{burc_workload_user}
1549	(project-wide convention, single source of truth).
1550	
1551	=== Garrison-Destructive Model
1552	
1553	Every
1554	{busn_garrison}
1555	invocation wipes prior workload state on the node regardless of which
1556	{burp_investiture}
1557	or station user runs it.
1558	There is exactly one workload user per node, ever; node runtime state
1559	belongs to whoever last garrisoned it.
1560	Garrison subsumes the workload-account lifecycle: there is no separate
1561	verb to materialize, remove, or enumerate workload accounts — every
1562	garrison rebuilds from a clean slate.
1563	+
1564	Admin-side sshd configuration is out of garrison's scope.
1565	{busn_fenestrate}
1566	owns Windows OpenSSH `sshd_config` hardening; on Linux/Mac the
1567	operator manages sshd directly.
1568	
1569	=== Tabtarget Catalog
1570	
1571	Fourteen tabtargets cover regime configuration, fenestrate, garrison
1572	ceremony, and workload dispatch.
1573	PowerShell garrison and dispatch are deferred and not enumerated below.
1574	
1575	==== Regime Configuration
1576	
1577	[[bust_node_regime_list]]
1578	//axvo_tabtarget
1579	{bust_node_regime_list}::
1580	`buw-rnl` —
1581	List all {burn_regime} profiles under `.buk/rbmn_nodes/`.
1582	
1583	[[bust_node_regime_render]]
1584	//axvo_tabtarget
1585	{bust_node_regime_render}::
1586	`buw-rnr` —
1587	Render a single {burn_regime} profile
1588	(viceroyalty passed as positional argument).
1589	
1590	[[bust_node_regime_validate]]
1591	//axvo_tabtarget
1592	{bust_node_regime_validate}::
1593	`buw-rnv` —
1594	Validate a single {burn_regime} profile
1595	(viceroyalty passed as positional argument).
1596	
1597	[[bust_privilege_regime_list]]
1598	//axvo_tabtarget
1599	{bust_privilege_regime_list}::
1600	`buw-rpl` —
1601	List all {burp_regime} profiles for the current {burs_user}.
1602	
1603	[[bust_privilege_regime_render]]
1604	//axvo_tabtarget
1605	{bust_privilege_regime_render}::
1606	`buw-rpr` —
1607	Render a single {burp_regime} profile
1608	(investiture passed as positional argument).
1609	
1610	[[bust_privilege_regime_validate]]
1611	//axvo_tabtarget
1612	{bust_privilege_regime_validate}::
1613	`buw-rpv` —
1614	Validate a single {burp_regime} profile
1615	(investiture passed as positional argument).
1616	
1617	==== Fenestrate (Windows OpenSSH only)
1618	
1619	[[bust_fenestrate]]
1620	//axvo_tabtarget
1621	{bust_fenestrate}::
1622	`buw-jpF` —
1623	{busn_fenestrate} ceremony: hardens admin SSH trust and `sshd_config`
1624	on a Windows OpenSSH node (uniform; no shell-letter variant).
1625	Capital `F` marks this as a persistent-config-application tabtarget.
1626	
1627	==== Garrison (per shell-letter)
1628	
1629	[[bust_garrison_bash]]
1630	//axvo_tabtarget
1631	{bust_garrison_bash}::
1632	`buw-jpGb` —
1633	{busn_garrison} ceremony with native bash as the workload shell
1634	(Linux and macOS nodes).
1635	Capital `G` marks this as a persistent-config-application tabtarget.
1636	
1637	[[bust_garrison_cygwin]]
1638	//axvo_tabtarget
1639	{bust_garrison_cygwin}::
1640	`buw-jpGc` —
1641	{busn_garrison} ceremony with Cygwin bash as the workload shell
1642	(Windows nodes via Cygwin sshd).
1643	
1644	[[bust_garrison_wsl]]
1645	//axvo_tabtarget
1646	{bust_garrison_wsl}::
1647	`buw-jpGw` —
1648	{busn_garrison} ceremony with WSL bash as the workload shell
1649	(Windows nodes via WSL distribution `rbtww-main`).
1650	
1651	==== Workload Dispatch
1652	
1653	[[bust_knock]]
1654	//axvo_tabtarget
1655	{bust_knock}::
1656	`buw-jwk` —
1657	Probe workload reachability via key-only SSH;
1658	returns 0 if the workload account answers a no-op command.
1659	
1660	[[bust_command_file]]
1661	//axvo_tabtarget
1662	{bust_command_file}::
1663	`buw-jwc` —
1664	Run the contents of a local command file as the workload account;
1665	shell is determined by whichever shell-letter garrisoned the node.
1666	Captures stdout, stderr, and remote exit status to {burd_output_dir}.
1667	
1668	[[bust_interactive_session]]
1669	//axvo_tabtarget
1670	{bust_interactive_session}::
1671	`buw-jws` —
1672	Open an interactive SSH session to the workload account.

file.content:
== Remote Node Access

BUK reaches remote nodes under the
{bus_jurisdiction}
umbrella: a single station user establishes admin SSH trust on the
node, provisions one workload account, and dispatches commands to it.

[[bus_jurisdiction]]
{bus_jurisdiction}::
The BUK feature-area for reaching remote nodes deterministically.
Two verbs cover the privileged-side ceremonies:
{busn_fenestrate}
hardens admin SSH trust and `sshd_config` on Windows OpenSSH nodes
(operator-manual on Linux/Mac, e.g., `ssh-copy-id`); {busn_garrison}
provisions and refreshes a workload account from an existing admin
SSH foothold. The workload-side tabtargets
({bust_knock}, {bust_command_file}, {bust_interactive_session})
dispatch work to that account.
+
{bus_jurisdiction}
is logical only — there is no AXLA-typed entity behind the umbrella.
Its implementation seat is
{bujb_jurisdiction}.

[[bujb_jurisdiction]]
{bujb_jurisdiction}::
The BCG-compliant module
(`Tools/buk/bujb_jurisdiction.sh` plus its `bujb_cli.sh` companion)
that houses both
{busn_fenestrate}
and
{busn_garrison}
implementations and hardcodes the
{bus_jurisdiction}
contracts not expressed in regime data:
the three shell-letter→`command=` directive mappings
(`b` = native bash for Linux/Mac;
`c` = Cygwin;
`w` = WSL)
used by garrison on the workload account,
the workload private-key destination paths on the remote per shell-letter,
the canonical WSL distribution name (`rbtww-main`),
and the Windows OpenSSH `sshd_config` hardening directive set used by
fenestrate.
+
SSH transport mechanics live in code;
{burp_regime}
carries no shell selection.

=== Privileges

A
{burn_regime}
instance lives under `.buk/rbmn_nodes/<{burn_viceroyalty}>/` and is shared
across station users (git-transported); it carries node shape only and
no key material.
A
{burp_regime}
instance lives under `.buk/rbmu_users/<{burs_user}>/<{burp_investiture}>/`;
the
{burs_user}
owns its credential subtree exclusively, and operations write only
under the current station user's tree.

{busn_garrison}
authenticates as
{burp_privileged_user}
using
{burp_privileged_key_file}
by key alone; admin trust is established beforehand by
{busn_fenestrate}
on Windows (which uses a one-time password fallback on its own
phase-1 admin SSH session) or operator-manually on Linux/Mac.
Workload account is unprivileged, ssh-only, and named by
{burc_workload_user}
(project-wide convention, single source of truth).

=== Garrison-Destructive Model

Every
{busn_garrison}
invocation wipes prior workload state on the node regardless of which
{burp_investiture}
or station user runs it.
There is exactly one workload user per node, ever; node runtime state
belongs to whoever last garrisoned it.
Garrison subsumes the workload-account lifecycle: there is no separate
verb to materialize, remove, or enumerate workload accounts — every
garrison rebuilds from a clean slate.
+
Admin-side sshd configuration is out of garrison's scope.
{busn_fenestrate}
owns Windows OpenSSH `sshd_config` hardening; on Linux/Mac the
operator manages sshd directly.

=== Tabtarget Catalog

Fourteen tabtargets cover regime configuration, fenestrate, garrison
ceremony, and workload dispatch.
PowerShell garrison and dispatch are deferred and not enumerated below.

==== Regime Configuration

[[bust_node_regime_list]]
//axvo_tabtarget
{bust_node_regime_list}::
`buw-rnl` —
List all {burn_regime} profiles under `.buk/rbmn_nodes/`.

[[bust_node_regime_render]]
//axvo_tabtarget
{bust_node_regime_render}::
`buw-rnr` —
Render a single {burn_regime} profile
(viceroyalty passed as positional argument).

[[bust_node_regime_validate]]
//axvo_tabtarget
{bust_node_regime_validate}::
`buw-rnv` —
Validate a single {burn_regime} profile
(viceroyalty passed as positional argument).

[[bust_privilege_regime_list]]
//axvo_tabtarget
{bust_privilege_regime_list}::
`buw-rpl` —
List all {burp_regime} profiles for the current {burs_user}.

[[bust_privilege_regime_render]]
//axvo_tabtarget
{bust_privilege_regime_render}::
`buw-rpr` —
Render a single {burp_regime} profile
(investiture passed as positional argument).

[[bust_privilege_regime_validate]]
//axvo_tabtarget
{bust_privilege_regime_validate}::
`buw-rpv` —
Validate a single {burp_regime} profile
(investiture passed as positional argument).

==== Fenestrate (Windows OpenSSH only)

[[bust_fenestrate]]
//axvo_tabtarget
{bust_fenestrate}::
`buw-jpF` —
{busn_fenestrate} ceremony: hardens admin SSH trust and `sshd_config`
on a Windows OpenSSH node (uniform; no shell-letter variant).
Capital `F` marks this as a persistent-config-application tabtarget.

==== Garrison (per shell-letter)

[[bust_garrison_bash]]
//axvo_tabtarget
{bust_garrison_bash}::
`buw-jpGb` —
{busn_garrison} ceremony with native bash as the workload shell
(Linux and macOS nodes).
Capital `G` marks this as a persistent-config-application tabtarget.

[[bust_garrison_cygwin]]
//axvo_tabtarget
{bust_garrison_cygwin}::
`buw-jpGc` —
{busn_garrison} ceremony with Cygwin bash as the workload shell
(Windows nodes via Cygwin sshd).

[[bust_garrison_wsl]]
//axvo_tabtarget
{bust_garrison_wsl}::
`buw-jpGw` —
{busn_garrison} ceremony with WSL bash as the workload shell
(Windows nodes via WSL distribution `rbtww-main`).

==== Workload Dispatch

[[bust_knock]]
//axvo_tabtarget
{bust_knock}::
`buw-jwk` —
Probe workload reachability via key-only SSH;
returns 0 if the workload account answers a no-op command.

[[bust_command_file]]
//axvo_tabtarget
{bust_command_file}::
`buw-jwc` —
Run the contents of a local command file as the workload account;
shell is determined by whichever shell-letter garrisoned the node.
Captures stdout, stderr, and remote exit status to {burd_output_dir}.

[[bust_interactive_session]]
//axvo_tabtarget
{bust_interactive_session}::
`buw-jws` —
Open an interactive SSH session to the workload account.

#!/bin/bash
#
# Copyright 2026 Scale Invariant, Inc.
#
# Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
# you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
# You may obtain a copy of the License at
#
#     http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0
#
# Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
# distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
# WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
# See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
# limitations under the License.
#
# Author: Brad Hyslop <bhyslop@scaleinvariant.org>
#
# Recipe Bottle Windows Handbook - rbhw_top function

set -euo pipefail

test -z "${ZRBHW0_SOURCED:-}" || return 0
ZRBHW0_SOURCED=1

rbhw_top() {
  zrbhw_sentinel

  buc_doc_brief "Display Windows handbook orchestrator — full setup sequence"
  buc_doc_shown || return 0

  buh_section  "Windows Test Infrastructure Setup"
  buh_line     "Complete setup sequence for running Recipe Bottle tests on a Windows host."
  buyy_cmd_yawp "${ZRBHW_WSL_DISTRO}"; local -r z_wsl_distro_yelp="${z_buym_yelp}"
  buh_line "   Target WSL distro: ${z_wsl_distro_yelp}"
  buyy_cmd_yawp "${ZRBHW_DOCKER_CONTEXT}"; local -r z_docker_context_yelp="${z_buym_yelp}"
  buh_line "   Docker context:    ${z_docker_context_yelp}"
  buh_e
  buh_section  "Phase 1: SSH Reachability (BUK — operator-manual prerequisite)"
  buh_tt       "  1. Jurisdiction handbook walkthrough:  " "${BUWZ_HJ0_TOP}"
  buh_tt       "  2. SSH client key generation:          " "${BUWZ_HW_ACCESS_REMOTE}"
  buh_e
  buh_section  "Phase 2: Admin Trust + sshd Harden (BUK — jurisdiction)"
  buh_tt       "  3. Fenestrate (admin SSH + harden):    " "${BUWZ_JP_FENESTRATE}" "" " <investiture>"
  buh_e
  buh_section  "Phase 3: Environments (BUK — generic OS install)"
  buh_tt       "  4. WSL distribution setup:             " "${BUWZ_HW_ENV_WSL}"
  buh_line "     Pass distro name: ${z_wsl_distro_yelp}"
  buh_tt       "  5. Cygwin installation:                " "${BUWZ_HW_ENV_CYGWIN}"
  buh_e
  buh_section  "Phase 4: Workload Provisioning (BUK — jurisdiction)"
  buh_tt       "  6. Garrison-Cygwin (workload):         " "${BUWZ_JP_GARRISON_CYGWIN}" "" " <investiture>"
  buh_tt       "  7. Garrison-WSL (workload):            " "${BUWZ_JP_GARRISON_WSL}" "" " <investiture>"
  buh_e
  buh_section  "Phase 5: User Provisioning (JJK — fundus accounts)"
  buh_line     "  8. Fundus user provisioning runs inside WSL:"
  buh_tt       "     Phase 1 (create users):             " "${JJZ_FUNDUS_PHASE1}"
  buh_tt       "     Phase 2 (clone repos):              " "${JJZ_FUNDUS_PHASE2}"
  buh_e
  buh_section  "Phase 6: Docker (RBK — project-specific)"
  buh_tt       "  9. Docker Desktop install:             " "${RBZ_HW_DOCKER_DESKTOP}"
  buh_tt       " 10. Native dockerd in WSL:              " "${RBZ_HW_DOCKER_WSL_NATIVE}"
  buh_line "     Pass distro name: ${z_wsl_distro_yelp}"
  buh_tt       " 11. Docker context discipline:          " "${RBZ_HW_DOCKER_CONTEXT}"

}

# eof

content.text:
# AAm Implementation Review — `implement-jurisdiction` (₢A-AAm)

## Executive verdict

**AAm meets its docket.** All five layers (L1–L5) land correctly. Verification is green within the documented baseline. Two structural docket-vs-source divergences and one BCG-discipline finding are surfaced below; none are bugs, but they are worth acknowledging before wrap.

---

## 1. Per-layer verdict

### L1 — Regime & Constants ✓ MEETS

Evidence:
- `Tools/buk/burp_regime.sh:40-47` enrolls the four-field shape: `BURP_VICEROYALTY` (xname 1-64), `BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER`, `BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE`, `BURP_WORKLOAD_KEY_FILE`. No `BURP_NODE/USER/SSH_PUBKEY/KEY_FILE/COMMAND` survivors.
- `Tools/buk/burc_regime.sh:64` enrolls `BURC_WORKLOAD_USER` as xname 1-32 in a "Jurisdiction" group.
- `Tools/buk/bubc_constants.sh:37-39` defines `BUBC_platforms_{linux,mac,windows}` as `bubep_*` literal tinder constants.
- `Tools/buk/burn_regime.sh:44` re-enrolls `BURN_PLATFORM` with `bubep_linux bubep_mac bubep_windows`. `BURN_WORKLOADS` is gone.
- Codebase-wide grep confirms zero remaining `BURP_NODE/USER/SSH_PUBKEY/KEY_FILE/COMMAND` or `BURN_WORKLOADS` references in `Tools/buk` or `Tools/rbk`.

### L2 — Module & Helper ✓ MEETS (with one structural divergence)

Evidence:
- `Tools/buk/bujb_jurisdiction.sh:126-139` `bujb_resolve_investiture` is the sole entrypoint; sentinels chain (`zbujb`+`zburc`+`zburn`+`zburp`); single-call guard via `ZBUJB_RESOLVED`; `zbujb_check_key_file` enforces exists + mode `0600` + `ssh-keygen -y -P ''` dry-load.
- The L3d cleanup commit (`7ec14b3b`) genuinely landed: no `BUJB_RESOLVED_*` symbols anywhere — call sites read `BURP_*`/`BURN_*`/`BURC_*` directly. `bujb_resolve` diagnostic at `bujb_cli.sh:43-50` displays the regime var names.
- L3e tinder relocation (`a650bf0c`) is in place: `BUJB_command_{b,c,w}`, `BUJB_workload_keypath_{b,c,w}`, `BUJB_wsl_distribution`, `BUJB_sshd_hardening` are bare assignments at module top level (`bujb_jurisdiction.sh:43-61`); `zbujb_kindle` (lines 66-79) holds only `BURD_TEMP_DIR`-dependent `ZBUJB_FENESTRATE_*` paths under `readonly`.
- Cross-validation of `BURP_VICEROYALTY` against BURN registration is by file presence in `bujb_cli.sh:222-227` (the furnish step before kindle), not in the helper. Matches docket intent ("the cli's BURP and BURN sourcing also implicitly cross-validates").

**Divergence**: Docket bullet "Operational tabtargets call `bujb_resolve_investiture` first, then assert per-verb platform invariant inline (2-line check; helper stays generic)." Implementation puts the platform assert *inside* the public verb function (`bujb_fenestrate` calls `zbujb_fenestrate_assert_platform` at `bujb_jurisdiction.sh:722`; `bujb_garrison` calls `zbujb_garrison_assert_platform` at `:467`), and the helpers are per-verb (not generic). CLI commands (`bujb_cli.sh:120-157`) just call resolve then dispatch. Behaviorally equivalent; structurally different shape from the docket. Functionality is unchanged — fenestrate still requires `bubep_windows`, garrison-b still allows `{bubep_linux,bubep_mac}`, garrison-c/w still requires `bubep_windows`.

### L3 — Tabtargets & Ceremonies ✓ MEETS

14 tabtargets present in `tt/`, all enrolled in `Tools/buk/buwz_zipper.sh`:

| Tabtarget | Channel | Module function |
|-----------|---------|-----------------|
| `buw-rnl/rnr/rnv` | `""`/`param1`/`param1` | burn_list/render/validate |
| `buw-rpl/rpr/rpv` | `""`/`param1`/`param1` | burp_list/render/validate |
| `buw-jpF` | `param1` | bujb_fenestrate_command |
| `buw-jpGb/c/w` | `param1` | bujb_garrison_{bash,cygwin,wsl} |
| `buw-jwk/jwc/jws` | `param1` | bujb_knock/command_file/interactive_session |
| `buw-hj0` | `""` | buhj_top |

- `buw-jwk` exports `BURD_NO_LOG=1` (probe target — captured via tail elsewhere).
- Fenestrate two-phase contract faithfully implemented: `zbujb_fenestrate_phase1` (chunk A: pubkey + icacls + sshd_config merge + `sshd -t` + `Get-Content -Raw` last; chunk B: `Restart-Service sshd` with exit ignored via `|| true`); `zbujb_fenestrate_phase2` reconnects after `sleep 3` and re-emits `Get-Content`. `zbujb_fenestrate_verify_directives` parses with array load + case-glob + parameter expansion only — no awk/grep/tr.
- Garrison 6-step ceremony lands at `bujb_jurisdiction.sh:461-479`. Step 1 admin-SSH failure surfaces a platform-aware pointer (`fenestrate handbook` for windows, `ssh-copy-id` for non-windows) at lines 252-261 — matches docket precisely.

### L4 — Spec & Integration ✓ MEETS (with one spec-vs-source mismatch — see §3)

- BUS0 `Remote Node Access` section (lines 1474-1672) covers two regimes (BURN/BURP), two verbs (Fenestrate/Garrison), garrison-destructive model (1551-1567), cross-cutting premises (`burn_host_singularity`, `bus_keys_operator_owned`), and the 14-target catalog (1569-1672).
- BUS0 mapping section: `:burp_viceroyalty:` at line 133 (no `:burp_node:` survivors); sub-letter legend at lines 30-34 declares `bujb_` with b=bash/p=powershell monosemy under Quoin Sub-Letter Discipline; `:burn_platform:` carries `bubep_*` enumeration in its definition.
- BUSJG{B,C,W}/BUSJPF/BUSJWK/BUSJWC/BUSJWS/BUSJH0 step bodies match the implemented 6-step garrison / 2-phase fenestrate / knock / command-file / interactive-session contracts.
- Colophon retirement landed: `tt/buw-HWab` and `tt/buw-HWax` are gone; zero references to `BUWZ_HW_ACCESS_BASE/ENTRY` or `buhw_access_base/entrypoints` in `buwz_zipper.sh` or `buhw_windows.sh`. `BUWZ_HW_ACCESS_REMOTE` correctly preserved (operator-owned client key generation, deferred per `bus_keys_operator_owned`).
- RBK Windows orchestrator rewire (`Tools/rbk/rbh0/rbhw0_top.sh`) cleanly lays out 6 phases / 11 steps. All 11 referenced zipper constants exist (`BUWZ_HJ0_TOP`, `BUWZ_HW_ACCESS_REMOTE`, `BUWZ_JP_FENESTRATE`, `BUWZ_HW_ENV_WSL`, `BUWZ_HW_ENV_CYGWIN`, `BUWZ_JP_GARRISON_CYGWIN`, `BUWZ_JP_GARRISON_WSL`, `JJZ_FUNDUS_PHASE1/2`, `RBZ_HW_DOCKER_DESKTOP`, `RBZ_HW_DOCKER_WSL_NATIVE`, `RBZ_HW_DOCKER_CONTEXT`). Phase ordering is correct: SSH reachability → admin trust+harden → environments → workload → user → docker.

### L5 — Verification ✓ MEETS

- `bash -n` green on all 11 edited shell files (`bujb_jurisdiction.sh`, `bujb_cli.sh`, `burc_regime.sh`, `burn_regime.sh`, `burp_regime.sh`, `bubc_constants.sh`, `buwz_zipper.sh`, `buhw_windows.sh`, `buhj_jurisdiction.sh`, `buhj_cli.sh`, `rbhw0_top.sh`).
- `tt/buw-st.BukSelfTest.sh` → `Suite 'self-test' passed (5 fixtures, 28 cases)`.
- `tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.fast.sh` → enrollment-validation 47/47 + regime-validation 25/26 = 72 passed / 1 failed. The lone failure is `rbtdrf_rv_rbrv_all_vessels`, exactly the pre-existing case noted as the BBAAz baseline (commit `945807a3`). No regression.

---

## 2. BCG compliance findings

**One finding worth flagging on bujb_jurisdiction.sh; the rest of the BCG audit is clean.**

### Finding 1 — `stat 2>/dev/null` on a local bash external (not in exception list)

`Tools/buk/bujb_jurisdiction.sh:96-98`:
```
if z_mode=$(stat -f '%A' "${z_path}" 2>/dev/null); then
  : # macOS / BSD stat
elif z_mode=$(stat -c '%a' "${z_path}" 2>/dev/null); then
  : # GNU/Linux stat
```

The docket's BCG axis exempts only `ssh`, `base64`, and `ssh-keygen` from `2>/dev/null`. `stat` is neither in that list nor a bash builtin. The pattern is BSD-then-GNU fallback, which is the kind of cross-platform try/catch the BCG line-1690 tension contemplates — but it's still a literal violation of the axis as worded. Two options for AAn or wrap-time decision:

- (a) Accept and document as a bona-fide ssh-keygen-class exception (cross-platform stat dance is a recognized pattern), OR
- (b) Refactor to a single `stat` with a try-os-detection-first guard (more code; same observable behavior).

I would lean (a) — the pattern is functionally identical to the cross-platform fallbacks the BCG already permits and the comment explains the intent. Surface the call out either way.

### Confirmed clean (post-AAm state)

- No `trap` on the local station (heredoc `trap` lines at `bujb_jurisdiction.sh:416,426` use `\$` escaping — they execute on the remote shell, not the operator's bash).
- No `awk`/`grep`/`tr`/`sed` anywhere in `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` or `bujb_cli.sh` (verified empty grep result).
- No `$(...)` capture on `ssh` calls — all ssh stdout/stderr land on disk via `> "${ZBUJB_FENESTRATE_*}"` redirection.
- Multi-`local` per line: zero violations (parallel grep returned empty).
- `readonly VAR=value` canonical form throughout `zbujb_kindle`.
- Tinder/kindle correctness: pure literals at module top level (`BUJB_command_*`, `BUJB_workload_keypath_*`, `BUJB_wsl_distribution`, `BUJB_sshd_hardening` lines 43-61, no `readonly`); runtime-dependent `ZBUJB_FENESTRATE_*` exclusively in `zbujb_kindle` with `readonly`.
- Heredocs into ssh stdin: limited to the established garrison-step + fenestrate-phase pattern flagged in commit `20eef3b9`. No new violations.
- `2>/dev/null` inside heredocs (lines 276, 277, 286, 287, 293, 294) execute on the *remote* shell — outside local BCG scope.

---

## 3. Spec-vs-source mismatches

### Finding 2 — `command=` directive strings are NOT in the spec, despite docket claim

Docket L4 bullet: "BUS0 / BUSJGB / BUSJGC / BUSJGW specify the three concrete `command=` directive strings (b/c/w) as locked spec content, sourced by the module rather than invented there."

Reality: the literal directive strings live only in `bujb_jurisdiction.sh:43-45` (the `BUJB_command_{b,c,w}` tinder constants). The four spec files (`BUSJGB/C/W` and BUS0) do **not** contain the literal `command="..."` text. Each just says e.g. "with the shell-letter `c` `command=` directive from `{bujb_jurisdiction}`" (`BUSJGC.adoc:48`).

So the spec delegates to the module rather than locking the literal strings. The module is the single source of truth. This is a defensible architectural choice — spec carries the *contract* (one directive per shell-letter, sourced from the module) and the module carries the *value*. But the docket bullet asserts the opposite (spec-as-content, module-as-consumer). Either:

- (a) Update the docket to match reality before wrap (spec specifies the *existence + sourcing arrangement*, module owns the literals), OR
- (b) Inline the three literal `command="..."` strings into BUS0/BUSJG{B,C,W} as locked spec content, with `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` documented to mirror.

Functionally indistinguishable today; the discipline question is whether the spec needs to lock the literal value or just the contract shape. I would lean (a) — the current arrangement matches the broader "implementation seat houses contracts not expressed in regime data" framing established in BUS0:1498-1517.

---

## 4. Catalog gaps

**None.** All 14 enumerated tabtargets exist with correct channels and zipper enrollments. The two retired colophons (`buw-HWab`, `buw-HWax`) are physically absent. The spec catalog (BUS0:1569-1672) enumerates exactly the 14 with matching `bust_*` linked terms.

---

## 5. Out-of-scope leakage

**None.**

- PowerShell garrison: not present. The `powershell -NoProfile -File -` invocations at `bujb_jurisdiction.sh:518,539` are the SSH-side dispatch shell for fenestrate (Windows OpenSSH default shell is cmd.exe, so PS is invoked explicitly to feed the heredoc) — fenestrate is in scope. No `bujp_jurisdiction.ps1` module exists; no `buw-jpGp` tabtarget exists.
- Adopt verb: not present.
- User regime: not present.
- Cygwin/WSL sshd hardening verb: not present (`zbujb_fenestrate_assert_platform` rejects anything other than `bubep_windows` at line 494).
- Broad cleanup sweep: held in scope. Pre-existing `tt/buw-hw` rot (BUWZ_RHC_LINUX/MAC/LOCALHOST, BUWZ_HW_VERIFY_SSH, BUWZ_RN_INSTALL_KEY) confirmed dormant in `buhw_windows.sh:197` and properly deferred to AAn per docket.
- E2E Windows test: not attempted; correctly reserved for AAD/E/F.

---

## 6. Pre-existing rot — confirmed dormant, not regressed

`tt/buw-hw.HandbookWindows.sh` fails under `set -u` at `Tools/buk/buhw_windows.sh:197` with `BUWZ_RHC_LINUX: unbound variable` — exactly as the L4 colophon-retirement commit message described. The L4 pruning didn't change the failure mode (only retired BUWZ_HW_ACCESS_BASE/ENTRY which are upstream of line 197). Properly deferred to AAn. ✓

---

## 7. Open questions worth surfacing before wrap

1. **Spec-vs-source on `command=` literals (Finding 2)** — does the spec lock the literal strings, or does it lock the contract and delegate to the module? Pick one and align the docket. Current implementation is the latter; docket asserts the former.
2. **Per-verb platform assertion location (L2 divergence)** — docket says inline at the tabtarget; implementation puts it inside the public verb function. Behavior is identical, but if the docket "helper stays generic" wording is load-bearing, the assertion should be inlined to the CLI command body and the per-verb assert helpers replaced with a single `zbujb_assert_platform LETTER VERB` (or the docket should be updated to acknowledge the per-verb-helper-inside-public-function arrangement). I lean toward the latter — the per-verb helpers are clearer than a 2-line inline check.
3. **`stat 2>/dev/null` (Finding 1)** — accept as a recognized cross-platform pattern (and document the exception expansion), or refactor.

None of the three blocks wrap; they're "is the docket bullet still true after the implementation arrived at its actual shape" questions.

---

## Recommendation

AAm is implementation-complete and verification-green within baseline. The two structural divergences (Findings 2 + per-verb assert location) are cleanups to either the docket or the source — not bugs. Finding 1 is a discipline-call. **Wrap-ready** once the operator decides on the three open questions; none require code changes for correctness.

Read-only review complete. No edits made, no commits, no wrap.

content.content:
17:Enterprise bash isn't about looking clever; it's about being boringly, reliably correct. These patterns optimize for the 3am debugging session, not the initial writing experience. Each external process spawn is a potential failure point, so bash builtins are preferred despite verbosity.
25:This is the general decision criterion that tools like Zeroes Theory instantiate. Zeroes Theory asks "how many zeroes did this add to state space?" — load-bearing complexity asks the prior question: "does this element earn its existence at all?"
46:**Rule**: Code outside the BCG module system must never call `z*_kindle()` directly. All external access to BCG modules goes through CLI scripts.
73:**What counts as "external code"**:
113:Furnish sequence: kindle → enforce → lock (ironclad gate before any command runs). After enforce succeeds, lock all enrolled `PREFIX_*` variables with `readonly` to prevent accidental mutation by downstream code. Any derived state (e.g., docker env arrays) must be built from validated values after enforce, before or during the lock step.
127:| `z«prefix»_kindle`   | Implementation | `test -z "${Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED:-}" \|\| buc_die`    | Yes (credentials) | No (use buc_log_*)| Define all kindle constants, set module state    |
134:**Kindle constant**: Any variable — internal (`Z«PREFIX»_SCREAMING_NAME`) or public (`«PREFIX»_SCREAMING_NAME`) — defined exclusively within the kindle function with `readonly`. No other function — including enroll, setup, or helper functions — may assign to kindle constants. This ensures module state is fully determined at kindle time and visible in one place.
136:**Mutable kindle state**: A variable initialized in kindle but intentionally mutated after kindle returns (counters, accumulators, registry rolls, builder state). Uses **lowercase** `z_«prefix»_name` to visually distinguish from `readonly` kindle constants. Never apply `readonly` to mutable kindle state.
140:**KINDLED must be last**: `readonly Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED=1` must be the final statement in kindle. Since sentinel checks this variable, setting it last guarantees all kindle constants, roll arrays, and enroll calls are complete before the module is considered operational. Any function calling sentinel before kindle finishes will correctly fail. The `readonly` makes re-kindling a loud error.
267:- The `case` statement can be omitted if a module has no conditional dependencies
380:# ✅ Kindle constant: depends on runtime state, requires kindle
452:test "${z_count}" -ge 0 2>/dev/null || buc_die "z_count must be non-negative integer, got: ${z_count}"
454:# ✅ Two-line pattern for captured values (explicit and debuggable)
456:z_token=$(zauth_get_token_capture) || buc_die "Failed to capture token"
458:# ❌ NEVER combine declaration with capture
459:local z_token=$(zauth_get_token_capture)  # Hides exit status!
471:z_token=$(zauth_get_token_capture) || buc_die "Failed to capture token"
502:All external command output capture uses temp files. The three prohibited `$()` patterns are: `local z_var=$(cmd)` (local swallows exit status), pipelines inside `$()` (hidden intermediate failures), and unguarded `$()` (silent failure). The only `$()` permitted on functions is for `_capture` (secrets that must not touch disk) and `_recite` (read-only roll access) — both with explicit `|| buc_die` or `|| return 1`. Bash introspection (`compgen`, `declare -F`, `mktemp`) is also permitted in `$()` as no file-based alternative exists.
516:# ✅ Correct: single external command via temp file
521:# ⚠️ EXCEPTION: Secrets use _capture functions, never temp files
529:Never use `2>/dev/null` on external commands. Redirect stderr to a temp file so forensic evidence is preserved when commands fail. Include the stderr file path in the `buc_die` message so the user can inspect it.
533:curl -sS "${z_url}" -o "${z_output_file}" 2>/dev/null \
536:# ✅ Correct: stderr captured to temp file, referenced in die message
603:# Exception 3: Two-line capture pattern (readonly would lock empty value)
605:z_token=$(zauth_get_token_capture) || buc_die "Failed to capture token"
607:# Exception 4: Accumulators and state-tracking flags
643:Some kindle functions initialize mutable state alongside constants — counters, accumulators, registry rolls, and builder state that are modified after kindle returns. These variables **must not** be `readonly`, but they must be visually distinguishable from kindle constants.
645:**Convention**: Mutable kindle state uses **lowercase** `z_«prefix»_name`. Constants use **UPPERCASE** `readonly Z«PREFIX»_NAME`. Case alone signals mutability.
654:  # ✅ Mutable kindle state — lowercase, NO readonly
657:  z_«prefix»_current_scope=""           # builder state set during enrollment
664:- Mutable kindle state follows the same `z_` internal prefix as roll arrays
665:- Never apply `readonly` to mutable kindle state
667:- The `_roll` suffix is conventional for parallel-array registries but not required for all mutable state (counters, flags, builder state use descriptive names)
669:**Test reset**: When tests need fresh enrollment state, provide an internal reset function that clears mutable kindle state without touching `KINDLED`. Re-kindling a module is never correct — the sentinel is `readonly`.
672:# ✅ Test support: reset mutable state, module stays kindled
677:  # ... clear all mutable kindle state ...
704:# Derived state built from validated values:
708:**Ordering matters**: Build derived state (docker env arrays, rollup strings) *after* enforce but *before* or *during* the lock call. Once variables are readonly, derived state captures validated, immutable values.
718:| Predicate | `[z]«prefix»_«name»_predicate` | `z«prefix»_sentinel` | 0=true, 1=false              | No (use buc_log_«source») | Never dies, status only             |
719:| Capture   | `[z]«prefix»_«name»_capture`   | `z«prefix»_sentinel` | stdout once at end or exit 1 | No (use buc_log_«source») | Clean error handling, single return |
723:| Tcase     | `«tc»_«name»_tcase`           | —                    | — (exit status to suite)     | No                        | Case subshell; assertions, verification |
735:z«prefix»_«name»_capture() {
751:z_value=$(z«prefix»_«name»_capture) || buc_die "Failed to capture value"
754:**Use capture functions for:**
760:- Returns stdout once at end or exits with status 1
768:#### Predicate Functions (status only, no output)
778:  # Return status only - no output, no dies
791:**Purpose**: Populate parallel-array registries (rolls) during module initialization. Enroll functions solve the subshell problem: when a function both mutates shared state and returns a value, calling it in `$()` loses the mutations.
797:- Variable naming: `z_«prefix»_«name»_roll` for all roll arrays (internal state, initialized in kindle)
823:  declare -F "${z_handler}" >/dev/null || buc_die "enroll: handler not found: ${z_handler}"
850:- Enroll functions work exclusively with rolls (parallel arrays) — no other shared state mutation
854:- May use `buc_die` internally (unlike `_capture`)
886:# Caller uses two-line capture pattern
892:- Read-only: MUST NOT mutate roll arrays or any shared state
893:- Returns via echo (like capture functions) — safe to call in `$()`
894:- Returns 1 on not-found (like `_capture`) — never uses `buc_die` internally
913:The two-line comment is the per-function template: signature on line 1, mandatory capture pattern on line 2. The `→` arrow is a visual reminder that the caller must capture on the same line.
915:**Return variable**: `z_«module»_«group»` — mutable kindle state, initialized in kindle. One per function group. All `_yawp` functions in a group share the same return variable. The value is valid until the next `_yawp` call in the same group.
918:# In kindle — initialize mutable kindle state for yawp group
927:# Caller pattern — semicolon capture on same line
928:«prefix»_cmd_yawp "git status";  local -r z_cmd="${z_«module»_«group»}"
933:- Sets `z_«module»_«group»` (mutable kindle state, initialized in kindle)
934:- **Caller MUST capture to `local -r` on the same line** — semicolon-separated, value is destroyed by the next `_yawp` call in the same group
935:- Pure bash builtins only — no external commands, no process spawning
944:# CORRECT — semicolon capture on same line, compose from captured locals
945:«prefix»_cmd_yawp "git status";  local -r z_cmd="${z_«module»_«group»}"
950:«prefix»_cmd_yawp "git status"
952:# ↑ Nothing prevents inserting code between yawp and capture.
956:«prefix»_cmd_yawp "git status"
959:# WRONG — two yawps before capture (first value lost)
960:«prefix»_cmd_yawp "git status"
965:The semicolon makes the capture discipline visually self-enforcing — you cannot accidentally insert code between the yawp and its capture. The two-line form invited interleaving mistakes; the one-line form eliminates them structurally.
967:The one-yawp-one-capture rhythm is the invariant. Pre-captured constants (e.g., `RBYC_*` from `zrbyc_kindle`) are safe to interpolate directly because they were captured at kindle time — the discipline applies at the yawp call site, not at the interpolation site.
976:- Functions that can fail → use `_capture`
979:- Functions that call external commands → use `_capture` or temp files
985:- **_capture**: Need a value that requires command substitution or clean error handling
1009:| Capture Internal functions   | `z«prefix»_«name»_capture`   | `zrbv_get_token_capture`     | Impl     | snake_case                    |
1010:| Capture Public functions     | `«prefix»_«name»_capture`    | `rbv_get_token_capture`      | Impl     | snake_case                    |
1025:| Yawp return variable         | `z_«module»_«group»`        | `z_buym_yelp`                | Impl     | snake_case, mutable kindle state |
1088:# ✅ Use exit-status predicates instead

500	### Temp Files Instead of Command Substitution
501	
502	All external command output capture uses temp files. The three prohibited `$()` patterns are: `local z_var=$(cmd)` (local swallows exit status), pipelines inside `$()` (hidden intermediate failures), and unguarded `$()` (silent failure). The only `$()` permitted on functions is for `_capture` (secrets that must not touch disk) and `_recite` (read-only roll access) — both with explicit `|| buc_die` or `|| return 1`. Bash introspection (`compgen`, `declare -F`, `mktemp`) is also permitted in `$()` as no file-based alternative exists.
503	
504	```bash
505	# ❌ Anti-pattern: nested command substitution
506	z_result=$(echo $(complex_command) | process)  # Hidden failures!
507	
508	# ❌ Anti-pattern: unguarded command substitution
509	z_timestamp=$(date +%s)  # Failure is silent!
510	
511	# ✅ Correct: temp files make failures visible afterward
512	complex_command > "${Z_MODULE_TEMP1}" || buc_die "Complex command failed"
513	process < "${Z_MODULE_TEMP1}" > "${Z_MODULE_TEMP2}" || buc_die "Process failed"
514	read -r z_result < "${Z_MODULE_TEMP2}" || buc_die "No result"
515	
516	# ✅ Correct: single external command via temp file
517	date +%s > "${Z_MODULE_TEMP1}" || buc_die "Failed to get timestamp"
518	local -r z_timestamp=$(<"${Z_MODULE_TEMP1}")
519	test -n "${z_timestamp}" || buc_die "Empty timestamp"
520	
521	# ⚠️ EXCEPTION: Secrets use _capture functions, never temp files
522	# ⚠️ EXCEPTION: _recite functions return via stdout (read-only roll access)
523	```
524	
525	**Temp file lifecycle**: Temp files under `BURD_TEMP_DIR` are **preserved after execution** for forensic debugging. Never delete temp files in module code — their persistence is intentional. Cleanup is handled by infrastructure outside BCG's scope.
526	
527	### Stderr Capture — Never Suppress
528	
529	Never use `2>/dev/null` on external commands. Redirect stderr to a temp file so forensic evidence is preserved when commands fail. Include the stderr file path in the `buc_die` message so the user can inspect it.
530	
531	```bash
532	# ❌ Anti-pattern: stderr suppressed — failure cause invisible
533	curl -sS "${z_url}" -o "${z_output_file}" 2>/dev/null \
534	  || buc_die "Failed to fetch ${z_url}"
535	
536	# ✅ Correct: stderr captured to temp file, referenced in die message
537	curl -sS "${z_url}" -o "${z_output_file}" 2>"${z_stderr_file}" \
538	  || buc_die "Failed to fetch ${z_url} — see ${z_stderr_file}"
539	```
540	
541	**Stderr file naming**: Use a kindle `_PREFIX` constant (grouped with other temp file prefixes for collision visibility) and append a discriminator at the usage site.
542	
543	**In loops**, use an auto-incrementing integer for uniqueness rather than trusting loop data values to be unique:
